behaviors.description: The generated reply references specific points from the incoming email rather than producing a generic response.
behaviors.justification: The failure mode is that the LLM ignores the email context and generates a generic response. A mocked LLM would hide this because the mock can be configured to always pass, regardless of whether the real LLM actually receives and processes the email context. Only a real LLM call verifies that context flows correctly and is actually used in the response.
behaviors.test_description: Call the system with a specific email thread containing unique details (names, dates, specific issues), generate a draft, and verify the draft contains those specific details verbatim or paraphrased. Use a real LLM to ensure context is passed and processed correctly.

behaviors.description: The factual consistency check flags claims in the draft that contradict entries in the knowledge base.
behaviors.justification: The failure mode is that a contradictory claim is not flagged. Testing this requires extracting claims from the draft and looking them up in a real or realistic KB. While claim extraction could be deterministic (Unit), the KB lookup and contradiction detection need real or realistic KB behavior. A trivial mock would pass even if the actual KB integration is broken. If the contradiction detection uses an LLM, Agentic would be needed, but assuming logical/similarity-based checking, Integration is the minimum.
behaviors.test_description: Create a draft with a factually incorrect claim, populate a KB with contradictory information, run the consistency check, and verify it flags the contradiction. Use a real or realistic KB (e.g., in-memory database with realistic data), not a trivial mock that always returns success.

behaviors.description: When the knowledge base has no relevant entries, the draft includes a disclaimer rather than fabricating facts.
behaviors.justification: This behavior splits into two parts: (1) recognizing empty KB and adding a disclaimer (Integration-level), and (2) the LLM actually avoiding fabrication (Agentic-level). A mocked LLM would hide the real failure—that the LLM ignores the 'don't fabricate' instruction and makes up facts. Only a real LLM call can verify it follows the instruction when KB coverage is low. Agentic is the minimum to catch both aspects.
behaviors.test_description: Create a KB with limited, specific entries. Generate a draft on a topic poorly covered by the KB. Verify that: (a) a disclaimer appears in the draft, and (b) all factual claims in the draft are either in the KB or marked as inferred/speculative. Use a real LLM to test whether it actually avoids fabrication when instructed.

behaviors.description: The approval workflow blocks sending until the user explicitly approves — no draft is sent automatically.
behaviors.justification: This is a control-flow invariant test: email is sent only after approval is given. The failure mode is straightforward—send() happens without the approval check. This can be caught with a Unit test using mocked approval and email-sending mechanisms. No real infrastructure, LLM, or multi-step orchestration is needed; the test only verifies deterministic conditional logic.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the approval flag and email send function. Verify that send_email() is not called when approval=false. Verify that send_email() is called when approval=true. Test both positive and negative paths of the approval check.

behaviors.description: The full sequence — read thread, generate draft, check facts, present for approval — works end-to-end for a real email thread.
behaviors.justification: This test covers multiple agentic steps in sequence (draft generation LLM call + fact-checking, which may also use an LLM) plus System-level infrastructure (email reading from real storage). Mocking any step defeats the purpose. The failure modes span multiple layers: email reading errors, draft generation failures, fact-check bugs, approval logic issues, and sending failures. Only a Workflow-level test with real LLM calls across the full pipeline can catch integration failures.
behaviors.test_description: Set up a real email thread in test storage, run the complete pipeline (read email → generate draft → check facts → present for approval → send), and verify: (a) each stage produces correct output, (b) facts in the final draft are consistent with the KB, (c) email is not sent without approval, and (d) the full sequence completes without errors. Use real LLM calls for draft generation and fact-checking.
